title: Parameters and Arguments
subtitle: Pass data into your functions to make them flexible and reusable
learning_objectives: Define a function that accepts one or more parameters
Call a function with the correct number of arguments
Understand that parameters are local variables inside the function
Define and call a function with multiple parameters

sections.heading: Parameters vs Arguments
sections.content:
Parameters are the variable names listed inside the parentheses of a def statement. They act as placeholders for values that will be supplied later.

Arguments are the actual values you pass to the function when you call it. Python assigns each argument to the matching parameter in order.
sections.code_example:
# 'name' is a PARAMETER — a placeholder in the definition
def greet(name):
    print("Hello, " + name + "!")

# "Alice" is an ARGUMENT — the actual value passed in
greet("Alice")
greet("Bob")

sections.heading: Multiple Parameters
sections.content: A function can accept more than one parameter. Separate them with commas inside the parentheses. When you call the function, supply one argument for each parameter in the same order.
sections.code_example:
def add(a, b):
    print(a + b)

add(3, 5)
add(10, 20)

sections.heading: Parameters Are Local to the Function
sections.content: A parameter is a local variable — it exists only inside the function. Once the function finishes, the parameter is gone. This means you can use the same parameter name in different functions without any conflict.
sections.code_example:
def double(number):
    result = number * 2
    print(result)

def triple(number):
    result = number * 3
    print(result)

double(4)
triple(4)
# 'number' and 'result' from each function don't interfere with each other

exercises.title: Greet by Name
exercises.prompt: Define a function called greet_person that takes one parameter name and prints Hello, [name]!. Call it with your own name.
hints: def greet_person(name): — name is the parameter
Inside the function use print("Hello, " + name + "!")

exercises.title: Multiply Two Numbers
exercises.prompt: Define a function called multiply that takes two parameters a and b, and prints their product. Call it with the arguments 6 and 7.
exercises.starter_code: # Define multiply(a, b) and call it with 6 and 7
hints: def multiply(a, b): — two parameters separated by a comma

exercises.title: Introduce a Person
exercises.prompt: Define a function called introduce that takes two parameters name and age, and prints: My name is [name] and I am [age] years old. Call it with any name and age.
exercises.starter_code: # Define introduce(name, age) and call it
hints: Remember to convert age to a string with str(age) when joining with +
Or use an f-string: print(f"My name is {name} and I am {age} years old.")

quiz.question: What is the difference between a parameter and an argument?
options: A parameter is the variable in the function definition; an argument is the value passed when calling the function
They are exactly the same thing
An argument is the variable in the definition; a parameter is the value passed when calling
Parameters are only used in built-in functions
quiz.explanation: Parameters are placeholders defined in the function signature. Arguments are the concrete values supplied at call time. Python maps each argument to the corresponding parameter in order.

quiz.question:
What will the following code print?

def show(x, y):
    print(x - y)

show(10, 3)
quiz.explanation: x is assigned 10 and y is assigned 3. 10 - 3 equals 7, so 7 is printed.

completion_message: Excellent! You can now write functions that accept data through parameters, making them flexible enough to work with any values you pass in.
